text: How to solve problems using global conservation equations? 1. List what you know and what you want to find. 2. Draw your control volume such that surfaces are where you know information or you want to find information. 3. Decide whether you need to use conservation of mass, momentum or both. If you want to find force, you need to use at least conservation of momentum. 2-43
raw_text: How to solve problems using global conservation equations? 1. List what you know and what you want to find. 2. Draw your control volume such that surfaces are where you know information or you want to find information. 3. Decide whether you need to use conservation of mass, momentum or both. If you want to find force, you need to use at least conservation of momentum. 2-43

text: 4. List your assumptions, ie steady, \( \rho=000 \). incompressible, inviscid, \( \frac{5 \sim 0}{6,0} \), and determine which term if any are zero in conservation equations. 5. Determine \( \overrightarrow{\underline{u}}, \overrightarrow{\underline{u}} \cdot \vec{n} \), and \( \underline{p} \) on the surfaces of the control volume. 6. Apply the conservation equations and find unknowns. 2-44

text: Example 1: Thrust reverser on a jet engine Fully deployed thrust reverser on a cargo jet 2-45

text: Given: Find: drag force exerted off the engine on the plane Assumptions: by steady flow \( \frac{x(\cdots)}{\partial t}=0 \) no body forces uniform inlet and exit velocities no pressure and viscous forces 2-46 \( \vec{F}_{\text {body }}=0 \) \( P=0 \) \( \vec{F}_{\text {vics }}=0 \)

text: First, define a control volume (CV) which encloses the reverser. Then apply conservation of mass: \[ \frac{\partial}{\partial t} \oiiint_{V} \rho d V=-\oiint_{S} \rho \vec{u} \cdot \vec{n} d S \quad-\oiint_{S} \rho \vec{u} \cdot \vec{n} d S=0 \] \[ \begin{array}{l} \text { and conservation of momentum: } \end{array} \] \[ \begin{array}{l} +\oiiint_{V} \underset{\substack{(\sim 0 \text { body } \\ \text { ferest }}}{\rho} f_{\text {body }}^{b} d V+\vec{F}_{\text {ext }} \\ -\vec{F}_{\text {ext }}=-\oint_{S} \rho \vec{u}(\vec{u} \cdot \vec{n}) d S \end{array} \] 2-47

text: Cons. of Mass \[ -\iint_{S} \rho(\vec{u} \cdot \vec{n}) d S=-\iint_{S_{1}} \rho\left(u_{1} \hat{\imath} \cdot \vec{n}\right) d S_{1}-\iint_{S_{2}} \rho\left(\vec{u}_{2} \cdot \vec{n}\right) d S_{2}-\iint_{S_{3}} \rho\left(\vec{u}_{3} \cdot \vec{n}\right) d S_{3}=0 \] * Since we have uniform flow 1 to surfece at each surfece, use as shortcut \[ \begin{array}{l} \underbrace{\rho u_{1} A_{1}}_{m_{1}}-\underbrace{\rho u_{2} A_{2}}_{m_{2}}-\underbrace{\rho u_{3} A_{3}}_{m_{3}}=0 \quad \begin{array}{l} \dot{m}_{1}=\dot{m}_{2}+\dot{m}_{3}=200 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{s} \\ 2 \dot{m}_{2}=200 \mathrm{ks} / \mathrm{s} \end{array} \\ \dot{m}_{2}=\dot{m}_{3}=100 \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{s} \end{array} \] \begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|l|} \hline \multirow[t]{5}{*}{\begin{tabular}{l} ! ない \\ \( \begin{array}{ll}V_{3} & \vec{n}_{3}\end{array} \) \end{tabular}} & \multicolumn{3}{|l|}{\multirow{2}{*}{\begin{tabular}{l} Determir quatities \( C \) each surface. \\ S. \\ \( S_{2} \) \\ \( S_{3} \) \end{tabular}}} \\ \hline & & & \\ \hline & \( \vec{u}_{1}=v_{1} \widehat{\imath} \) & \( \vec{u}_{2}=\left\langle-V_{2} \cos 45^{\circ}, V_{2} \sin 45^{\circ}\right\rangle \) & \\ \hline & \( u_{1, x}=v_{1} \) & \( u_{2, x}=-v_{2} \cos 45^{\circ} \) & \( U_{3, x}=-V_{3} \cos 45^{\circ} \) \\ \hline & \( \vec{n}_{1}=-\hat{\imath} \) & \( \vec{n}_{2}=\left\langle-\cos 45^{\circ}, \sin 45^{\circ}\right\rangle \) & \( \vec{n}_{3}=\left\langle-\cos 45^{\circ},-\sin 45^{\circ}\right\rangle \) \\ \hline \end{tabular} 2-48

text: Conservation of Mass \( \frac{\partial}{\partial t} \oiint_{V} p d V=-\oiint_{S} p \vec{u} \cdot \vec{n} d S \Rightarrow 0 \) o (steady) At \( S_{1} \) \[ \begin{array}{l} \vec{u}_{1}=u_{c}\left(1-\frac{r^{2}}{R^{2}}\right) \hat{e}_{z} \\ \vec{n}_{1}=-\hat{e}_{z} \end{array} \] Su \[ \begin{array}{l} \vec{u}_{z}=u_{z} \hat{e}_{z} \\ \vec{n}_{z}=\hat{e}_{z} \end{array} \] \[ \oint_{S_{1}} \rho \vec{u}_{1} \cdot \vec{n}_{1} d S_{1}+\oint_{S_{2}} \rho \vec{u}_{2} \cdot \vec{n}_{2} d S_{2}=0 \] \[ \frac{A_{2}}{A_{1}}=\frac{1}{2} \frac{U_{c}}{U_{2}} \] We wat numerical value, So neel momatum + set \( u_{0} / u_{2} \). 2-51

text: A small hole is punched in the side of a jug of vinegar. If the velocity at the exit is \( v=(2 g h)^{1 / 2} \), determine the following: a.) The height of the vinegar versus time. Not b.) The time it takes to completely drain. Not stead \[ \varepsilon_{n d}(1)^{a / 2 / 2} \frac{\partial}{\partial t} \int_{V} \rho d V=\frac{\partial}{\partial t} \int_{0}^{2 D_{2} / 2} \int_{0}^{b} \int_{0}^{b} \rho z r d r d \theta=\frac{\partial}{\partial t}\left(\rho \pi \frac{D^{2}}{4} h\right)=\rho \pi \frac{D^{2}}{4} \frac{d h}{d t} \] (2.) Evelucte \( \oiint_{S} \vec{u} \) inds at each sufcre of c.v. (5) Everantion Neglighte \[ \vec{v} \cdot \vec{n} \approx 0 \] (S2) Solve wells \[ 2 \pi d \vec{k}_{2} \cdot \vec{n}=0 \] \( S_{3} \) Hale! \[ \vec{v} \cdot \vec{n}=\left(\sqrt{2 g h} \hat{e}_{r} \cdot \hat{e}_{r}\right)=\sqrt{2 g h} \] 2-35 \[ \begin{array}{l} \int_{S_{3}} \rho \vec{u} \cdot \vec{n} d S_{3}=\int_{0}^{2 \pi} \int_{0}^{d / 2} \sqrt{2 g h} r d r d \theta=\frac{\rho \pi d^{2}}{4} \sqrt{2 g h} \\ \rho \pi \frac{D^{2}}{d t} \frac{d h}{d t}+\frac{\rho^{2}+d^{2}}{2 g h}=0 \quad \frac{d h}{d t}=-\left(\frac{d}{D}\right)^{2} \sqrt{2 g h} \end{array} \]

text: Example 3 Find the force transmitted by the coupling between the nozzle and the hose. Given: \( \mathrm{D}_{1}=75 \mathrm{~mm}, \mathrm{D}_{2}=25 \mathrm{~mm}, \mathrm{P}_{1, \text { gage }}=500 \mathrm{kPa} \), \( P_{2}=P_{a}, V_{2}=36 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s} \) Missing \( V_{1} \) ? Use Consv. \& Mass First! \[ \begin{array}{l} \begin{array}{l} \frac{\partial}{\partial t} \int \rho d V=-\oiint_{S} \rho \vec{u} \cdot \vec{n} d S=0 \text { Hos to be steck so is wiform all } 1 \frac{\partial}{\partial k} \cdot 0 \\ \text { Flow tow } \end{array} \\ \text { surfaces, so we our shartent. } \\ \rho U_{1} A_{1}=\rho U_{2} A_{2} \in \text { Mcs In }=\text { Mcss Flow out } \\ u_{1}=u_{2} \frac{A_{2}}{A_{1}}=36 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}\left(\frac{\pi\left(\frac{25}{2}\right)^{2}}{\pi\left(\frac{75}{2}\right)^{2}}\right)=4 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s} \end{array} \] 2-53

text: Example 4: Force on a sphere submerged in a liquid (a.k.a. buoyancy in a more rigorous way!) Cons. of Mometer Simplifies to... \[ \vec{F}=-\oiint_{S}(p \vec{n}) d S \] \( p=p_{a}-\rho g\left(y-y_{0}\right) \) cartesia to iphencel.. \[ =\left(p a+p g y_{0}\right)-p g(R \cos \theta) \hat{e}_{r} \] \[ \vec{F}=-\oiint_{S} \underbrace{\left(P_{a}+p g_{0}^{\circ}\right)}_{\substack{\text { contative } \\ \text { By divester } \\ \text { thm }=0}} d S+\iint p g(R \cos \theta) \underbrace{R^{2} \sin \theta d \theta d \phi}_{\Delta S} \] 2-55 \[ \begin{aligned} = & \operatorname{eg}_{r}=\sin \theta \cos \phi e_{x}+\sin \theta \sin \phi e_{z} \\ & =\int_{0}^{2 \pi} \int_{0}^{\pi} \cos \theta \sin ^{2} \theta \cos ^{2 \pi} \phi d \theta d \hat{e}_{x}=0 \\ & +\int_{0}^{2 \pi} \int_{0}^{\pi} \cos \theta \sin ^{2} \theta \sin \phi d \theta d \phi \hat{e}_{z} \\ & \left.+\int_{0}^{2 \pi} \int_{0}^{\pi} \cos ^{2} \theta \sin \phi d \theta d \phi \hat{e}_{y}\right\} \\ & \vec{F}=\left(\frac{4}{3} \pi R^{3}\right) p g \end{aligned} \]
raw_text: Example 4: Force on a sphere submerged in a liquid (a.k.a. buoyancy in a more rigorous way!) Cons. of Mometer Simplifies to... \[ \vec{F}=-\oiint_{S}(p \vec{n}) d S \] \( p=p_{a}-\rho g\left(y-y_{0}\right) \) cartesia to iphencel.. \[ =\left(p a+p g y_{0}\right)-p g(R \cos \theta) \hat{e}_{r} \] \[ \vec{F}=-\oiint_{S} \underbrace{\left(P_{a}+p g_{0}^{\circ}\right)}_{\substack{\text { contative } \\ \text { By divester } \\ \text { thm }=0}} d S+\iint p g(R \cos \theta) \underbrace{R^{2} \sin \theta d \theta d \phi}_{\Delta S} \] 2-55 \[ \begin{aligned} = & \operatorname{eg}_{r}=\sin \theta \cos \phi e_{x}+\sin \theta \sin \phi e_{z} \\ & =\int_{0}^{2 \pi} \int_{0}^{\pi} \cos \theta \sin ^{2} \theta \cos ^{2 \pi} \phi d \theta d \hat{e}_{x}=0 \\ & +\int_{0}^{2 \pi} \int_{0}^{\pi} \cos \theta \sin ^{2} \theta \sin \phi d \theta d \phi \hat{e}_{z} \\ & \left.+\int_{0}^{2 \pi} \int_{0}^{\pi} \cos ^{2} \theta \sin \phi d \theta d \phi \hat{e}_{y}\right\} \\ & \vec{F}=\left(\frac{4}{3} \pi R^{3}\right) p g \end{aligned} \]

text: Conservation of Momentum for a CV with Rectilinear Acceleration The global momentum conservation for a stationary 1) \( \rho \stackrel{\rightharpoonup}{u} \) is the momentum (per unit volume) of fluid inside \( V \). It must be measured in inertial reference frame. 2) \( \rho \vec{u} \) is the momentum (per unit volume) of fluid crossing \( S \). Must be measured in inertial reference frame. 3) \( \vec{u} \cdot \vec{n} \) determines the rate at which fluid flows out of V. It is the velocity relative to \( S \). \( 2-56 \)
raw_text: Conservation of Momentum for a CV with Rectilinear Acceleration The global momentum conservation for a stationary 1) \( \rho \stackrel{\rightharpoonup}{u} \) is the momentum (per unit volume) of fluid inside \( V \). It must be measured in inertial reference frame. 2) \( \rho \vec{u} \) is the momentum (per unit volume) of fluid crossing \( S \). Must be measured in inertial reference frame. 3) \( \vec{u} \cdot \vec{n} \) determines the rate at which fluid flows out of V. It is the velocity relative to \( S \). \( 2-56 \)

text: For an accelerating CV the momentum conservation becomes: Let \( \vec{u}^{r f} \) be the velocity of the accelerating reference frame that moves with the CV. CV remains fixed in this reference frame. The velocity in the inertial and accelerating reference frames are related by \[ \vec{u}^{I}=\vec{u}^{r f}+\vec{u}^{r e l} \] Substituting we get: \[ \begin{array}{l} \frac{\partial}{\partial t} \oiiint_{V} \rho \vec{u}^{I} d V=\frac{\partial}{\partial t} \oiiint_{V} \rho \vec{u}^{r f} d V+\frac{\partial}{\partial t} \oiiint_{V} \rho \vec{u}^{r e l} d V \\ \quad=\vec{u}^{r f} \frac{\partial}{\partial t} \oiiint_{V} \rho d V+\frac{\partial\left(\vec{u}^{r f}\right)}{\partial t} \oiiint_{V} \rho d V+\frac{\partial}{\partial t} \oiiint_{V} \rho \vec{u}^{r e l} d V \end{array} \] 2-57
raw_text: For an accelerating CV the momentum conservation becomes: Let \( \vec{u}^{r f} \) be the velocity of the accelerating reference frame that moves with the CV. CV remains fixed in this reference frame. The velocity in the inertial and accelerating reference frames are related by \[ \vec{u}^{I}=\vec{u}^{r f}+\vec{u}^{r e l} \] Substituting we get: \[ \begin{array}{l} \frac{\partial}{\partial t} \oiiint_{V} \rho \vec{u}^{I} d V=\frac{\partial}{\partial t} \oiiint_{V} \rho \vec{u}^{r f} d V+\frac{\partial}{\partial t} \oiiint_{V} \rho \vec{u}^{r e l} d V \\ \quad=\vec{u}^{r f} \frac{\partial}{\partial t} \oiiint_{V} \rho d V+\frac{\partial\left(\vec{u}^{r f}\right)}{\partial t} \oiiint_{V} \rho d V+\frac{\partial}{\partial t} \oiiint_{V} \rho \vec{u}^{r e l} d V \end{array} \] 2-57

text: Also, \( u^{T}=\vec{u}^{r F}+\vec{u}^{r e l} \) \[ \begin{array}{c} \oiint_{S} \rho \vec{u}^{Y}\left(\vec{u}^{r e l} \cdot \vec{n}\right) d S=\oiint_{S} \rho \vec{u}^{r f}\left(\vec{u}^{r e l} \cdot \vec{n}\right) d S+\oiint_{S} \rho \vec{u}^{r e l}\left(\vec{u}^{r e l} \cdot \vec{n}\right) d S \\ =\vec{u}^{r f} \oint_{S} \rho\left(\vec{u}^{r e l} \cdot \vec{n}\right) d S+\oiint_{S} \rho \vec{u}^{r e l}\left(\vec{u}^{r e l} \cdot \vec{n}\right) d S \end{array} \] Global conservation of mass for moving CV: \( \star \frac{\partial}{\partial t} \oiiint_{V} \rho d V=-\oiint_{S} \rho\left(\vec{u}^{\text {rel }} \cdot \vec{n}\right) d S \quad \begin{array}{c}\text { Multiply } \\ \overrightarrow{u^{f}} \ldots . .\end{array} \) Let \( \quad \begin{array}{c}\frac{\partial\left(\vec{u}^{r f}\right)}{\partial t}=\vec{a}_{r}^{r f} \\ \text { Accetertion } \\ \text { Perce }\end{array} \) and let \( \oiiint_{V} \rho d V=M_{T} \) Total Totel Mass of the Control Volum 2-58
raw_text: Also, \( u^{T}=\vec{u}^{r F}+\vec{u}^{r e l} \) \[ \begin{array}{c} \oiint_{S} \rho \vec{u}^{Y}\left(\vec{u}^{r e l} \cdot \vec{n}\right) d S=\oiint_{S} \rho \vec{u}^{r f}\left(\vec{u}^{r e l} \cdot \vec{n}\right) d S+\oiint_{S} \rho \vec{u}^{r e l}\left(\vec{u}^{r e l} \cdot \vec{n}\right) d S \\ =\vec{u}^{r f} \oint_{S} \rho\left(\vec{u}^{r e l} \cdot \vec{n}\right) d S+\oiint_{S} \rho \vec{u}^{r e l}\left(\vec{u}^{r e l} \cdot \vec{n}\right) d S \end{array} \] Global conservation of mass for moving CV: \( \star \frac{\partial}{\partial t} \oiiint_{V} \rho d V=-\oiint_{S} \rho\left(\vec{u}^{\text {rel }} \cdot \vec{n}\right) d S \quad \begin{array}{c}\text { Multiply } \\ \overrightarrow{u^{f}} \ldots . .\end{array} \) Let \( \quad \begin{array}{c}\frac{\partial\left(\vec{u}^{r f}\right)}{\partial t}=\vec{a}_{r}^{r f} \\ \text { Accetertion } \\ \text { Perce }\end{array} \) and let \( \oiiint_{V} \rho d V=M_{T} \) Total Totel Mass of the Control Volum 2-58

text: Now the global momentum conservation for moving CV is Global conservation of mass \[ \frac{\partial}{\partial t} \oiiint_{V} \rho d V=-\oiint_{S} \rho\left(\vec{u}^{r e l} \cdot \vec{n}\right) d S \] 2-59 End 9/26/25
raw_text: Now the global momentum conservation for moving CV is Global conservation of mass \[ \frac{\partial}{\partial t} \oiiint_{V} \rho d V=-\oiint_{S} \rho\left(\vec{u}^{r e l} \cdot \vec{n}\right) d S \] 2-59 End 9/26/25

text: \[ \begin{array}{l} \int_{h_{0}}^{h} \frac{d h}{\sqrt{h}}=-\int_{0}^{t}\left(\frac{d}{D}\right)^{2} \sqrt{2 y} d t \\ h(t)=\left(\sqrt{h_{0}}-\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{d}{D}\right)^{2} \sqrt{2 y} t\right)^{2} \end{array} \] Set \( h\left(t_{\text {end }}\right)=0 \) \( t_{\text {end }}=52.1 \) seconds (B.)

text: Conservation of momentum 2-36
raw_text: Conservation of momentum 2-36

text: Applied Forces: 2 kinds 1. Body forces: act throughout \( V \) (e.g., gravity, electromagnetic forces) 2. Surface force: act only on \( S \), boundary of \( V \) (e.g., pressure and viscous stresses) Body force: let \( \vec{f}= \) body force per unit mass of fluid \[ \left[\begin{array}{l} \text { body force } \\ \text { on } \mathrm{V} \end{array}\right]=\oiiint_{V} \rho \vec{f} d V \] Eg. Gravity \( \vec{f}:-g \hat{f} \) where Surface forces: due to pressure and viscous stresses on the surface of \( V \). 2-37
raw_text: Applied Forces: 2 kinds 1. Body forces: act throughout \( V \) (e.g., gravity, electromagnetic forces) 2. Surface force: act only on \( S \), boundary of \( V \) (e.g., pressure and viscous stresses) Body force: let \( \vec{f}= \) body force per unit mass of fluid \[ \left[\begin{array}{l} \text { body force } \\ \text { on } \mathrm{V} \end{array}\right]=\oiiint_{V} \rho \vec{f} d V \] Eg. Gravity \( \vec{f}:-g \hat{f} \) where Surface forces: due to pressure and viscous stresses on the surface of \( V \). 2-37

text: Pressure force: \[ \left[\begin{array}{l} \text { pressure force } \\ \text { on } \mathrm{V} \end{array}\right]=-\oiint_{S} p \vec{n} d S_{\text {tangeneer }} \] Total viscous force on \( V=\vec{F}_{v i s} \) [more on this later in the course] Momentum density: \( \rho \vec{u} \) momentum per unit volume 2-38
raw_text: Pressure force: \[ \left[\begin{array}{l} \text { pressure force } \\ \text { on } \mathrm{V} \end{array}\right]=-\oiint_{S} p \vec{n} d S_{\text {tangeneer }} \] Total viscous force on \( V=\vec{F}_{v i s} \) [more on this later in the course] Momentum density: \( \rho \vec{u} \) momentum per unit volume 2-38

text: \[ \left[\begin{array}{l} \text { rate of momemtum } \\ \text { accumulation } \\ \text { in } V \end{array}\right]=\frac{\partial}{\partial t} \oiiint_{V} \rho \underset{\substack{\uparrow \\ \text { vector }}}{\vec{u}} d V \] \( \not \gtrless \) \[ \begin{array}{l} \text { Finally, gather all the terms: } \end{array} \] 2-39
raw_text: \[ \left[\begin{array}{l} \text { rate of momemtum } \\ \text { accumulation } \\ \text { in } V \end{array}\right]=\frac{\partial}{\partial t} \oiiint_{V} \rho \underset{\substack{\uparrow \\ \text { vector }}}{\vec{u}} d V \] \( \not \gtrless \) \[ \begin{array}{l} \text { Finally, gather all the terms: } \end{array} \] 2-39

text: Volume \( V \) is fixed \( \Rightarrow \) so \( \frac{\partial}{\partial t} \) can only ect on \( \rho \vec{u} \) \[ \frac{\partial}{\partial t} \oiiint_{V} \rho \vec{u} d V=\oiiint_{V} \frac{\partial}{\partial t}(\rho \vec{u}) d V \] Pressure term (apply gradient theorem): \[ -\oiint_{S} p \vec{n} d S=-\oiint_{V} \nabla p d V=-\oiiint_{V}\left(\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} \hat{\imath}+\frac{\partial p}{\partial y} \hat{\jmath}+\frac{\partial p}{\partial z} \hat{\imath}\right) d V \] Note: velocity is \( \vec{u}=\left(u_{1}, u_{2}, u_{3}\right)=\left\langle u_{x}, u_{y}, u_{z}\right\rangle \) cartesian... Consider \( i \)-th component momentum flow and apply divergence theorem: \[ -\oiint_{S} \underbrace{\rho u_{i}(\vec{u} \cdot \vec{n})}_{\vec{F}} d S=-\int_{V}\left(\nabla \cdot\left(p u_{i} \vec{u}\right)\right) d V \] \( \tau_{i} \)-th compunet of velocity 2-40
raw_text: Volume \( V \) is fixed \( \Rightarrow \) so \( \frac{\partial}{\partial t} \) can only ect on \( \rho \vec{u} \) \[ \frac{\partial}{\partial t} \oiiint_{V} \rho \vec{u} d V=\oiiint_{V} \frac{\partial}{\partial t}(\rho \vec{u}) d V \] Pressure term (apply gradient theorem): \[ -\oiint_{S} p \vec{n} d S=-\oiint_{V} \nabla p d V=-\oiiint_{V}\left(\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} \hat{\imath}+\frac{\partial p}{\partial y} \hat{\jmath}+\frac{\partial p}{\partial z} \hat{\imath}\right) d V \] Note: velocity is \( \vec{u}=\left(u_{1}, u_{2}, u_{3}\right)=\left\langle u_{x}, u_{y}, u_{z}\right\rangle \) cartesian... Consider \( i \)-th component momentum flow and apply divergence theorem: \[ -\oiint_{S} \underbrace{\rho u_{i}(\vec{u} \cdot \vec{n})}_{\vec{F}} d S=-\int_{V}\left(\nabla \cdot\left(p u_{i} \vec{u}\right)\right) d V \] \( \tau_{i} \)-th compunet of velocity 2-40

text: Approaches to fluid problems: 1. Global equations (integral form): - Useful for analysis of a fluid system as a whole - Solve when you need to know global quantities (lift, drag, total force) - Need to have information at the boundaries 2. Local equations (differential form): - Solve when you need to know detailed information (e.g., distribution of \( p \) over a surface) - Can integrate results to get global quantities - Need to have appropriate boundary conditions Start with global equations first. 2-42
raw_text: Approaches to fluid problems: 1. Local equations (differential form): - Solve when you need to know detailed information (e.g., distribution of \( p \) over a surface) - Can integrate results to get global quantities - Need to have appropriate boundary conditions Start with global equations first. 2-42
